front: Figure: a Cherokee woman was posthumously given the name "Talihina," meaning "railroad," by a newspaperman unfamiliar with its original pronunciation
Figure: gold was mined on this tribe's lands during an event called the "Great Intrusion"
Figure: one of the Five Civilized Tribes, with lands today mainly in western North Carolina and northeastern Oklahoma
Event: Sequoyah, inspired by "talking leaves" he saw while fighting at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend, single-handedly created an 86-character syllabary for the Cherokee language
Event: Cherokee literacy increased dramatically after this writing system's development, enabling the creation of religious texts
Event: this syllabary enabled the publication of the bilingual Cherokee newspaper called the Phoenix
Figure: US Senator Robert Latham Owen of Oklahoma was the great-grandson of a member of this tribe
Treaty: signed in 1835 by an unauthorized Cherokee faction, ceding all southeastern tribal territory in exchange for land west of the Mississippi
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Treaty: preceded by the Dahlonega Gold Rush in Georgia, which intensified pressure to remove the Cherokee
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Figure: the Cherokee's longest-serving Principal Chief, who opposed the ceding of southeastern territory in the Treaty of New Echota
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Institution: unofficial Cherokee faction including Major Ridge, John Ridge, and Elias Boudinot that signed the Treaty of New Echota, feuding with Principal Chief John Ross
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Figure: was targeted for assassination for signing the 1835 Treaty of New Echota on behalf of the Cherokee, but survived
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Figure: this editor of the Cherokee Phoenix was assassinated for supporting the Treaty of New Echota
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Figure: leader of the Cherokee "Triumvirate" who feuded with a rival he called "Babykiller," Doublehead, after Doublehead ordered the killing of the Brown Party
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Figure: missionary who lived among the Cherokee without the required state license; his arrest prompted a landmark Supreme Court case
work: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split

front: Event: this forced Cherokee removal split into the mostly aquatic Deas-Whitely Route (from Blythe Ferry) and an overland route ending at Webbers Plantation; some who died along the way are buried at McGinnis Cemetery, Illinois
Figure: the "Migrant Mother" photographed by Dorothea Lange during the Great Depression was a member of this tribe
Figure: led the Chickamauga faction of the Overhill Cherokee in opposing the Watauga Association and raiding the State of Franklin
Event: 1794 expedition by white settlers that targeted Chickamauga Cherokee towns led by Dragging Canoe
Figure: while this man led the Cherokee, the tribe took care of teenage Sam Houston
Figure: held the title "Beloved Woman"; warned colonial settlers of raids planned by her cousin Dragging Canoe during the Revolutionary War
Figure: credited with introducing dairy farming and loom weaving to the Cherokee
Figure: became the last Confederate general to surrender in the field, at the end of the Civil War
Figure: captured the steamboat J.R. Williams and a wagon train near Cabin Creek before later surrendering at Doaksville
Concept: in this Cherokee creation myth, a water beetle dives down and retrieves mud that becomes the earth, later shaped into mountains and valleys by a buzzard's beating wings
Concept: after owls failed, this creature brought fire from a sycamore tree to the Cherokee people, carrying it in a basket on her back
Figure: Cherokee corn spirit who creates corn from her body, in variants by being dragged along the ground or having her stomach rubbed
Figure: gave his sons bows to hunt with after they accidentally released all the animals from a cave, in Cherokee myth
Figure: horned serpent in Cherokee myth whose forehead holds a shining crystal that grants supernatural powers to whoever steals it
Figure: giant hornet in Cherokee myth that nested and fought warriors on mountaintops until the Great Spirit blasted the mountains with lightning
Culture: humorous Cherokee ceremonial dance using carved gourd masks with phalluses to satirize outsiders such as white settlers
Culture: Cherokee ritual purification using a drink made from skullcap flower leaves, performed after contact with menstruating women
Culture: Cherokee winter ceremony involving emetics used over running water and tossing tobacco into a sacred fire
Event: 1730s Cherokee smallpox epidemic attributed to evil spirits called Kosvkv Askini; combated by modifying the purifying rite "Going-to-Water"
Figure: became the first woman elected Principal Chief of the Cherokee Nation; later received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1998
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Figure: was considered as a candidate to replace Andrew Jackson on the US $20 bill
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Document: 1898-1914 census of the Five Civilized Tribes whose "by blood" section Wilma Mankiller used to limit Cherokee Nation membership
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Event: descendants of people enslaved by Cherokee members like Stand Watie were long disenfranchised, requiring a CDIB card to vote under Ross Swimmer's order, until a 2017 court ruling granted their descendants tribal citizenship
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Figure: current Cherokee Nation Principal Chief who applauded the 2020 McGirt v. Oklahoma ruling and has clashed with Oklahoma governor Kevin Stitt
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Concept: controversial ancestry-fraction requirement used by groups like the United Keetoowah Band and Eastern Band to determine Cherokee identity
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Work: book by Circe Sturm that categorizes disputed claimants to Cherokee identity as "outtalucks" and "should-bes"
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Figure: this US Senator's past claims to Cherokee ancestry became a widely-scrutinized controversy
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation

front: Figure: Cherokee runner who won the 1928 "Bunion Derby" footrace along much of Route 66
work: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation
